content.body: Thermal physics deals with heat, temperature, and the energy stored in systems. These concepts are closely related but importantly different. Understanding these distinctions is essential for A-level physics.

Key definitions:
Temperature — a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a substance (measured in °C or K)
Heat (thermal energy) — energy transferred from a hotter to a cooler body
Internal energy — total kinetic and potential energy of all particles in a substance

Conversion: T(K) = T(°C) + 273
Absolute zero = 0 K = −273°C (particles have minimum energy)

content.title: Specific Heat Capacity
content.body:
Specific heat capacity (c) is the energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C (or 1 K).

Formula: Q = mcΔT
Where: Q = heat energy (J), m = mass (kg), c = specific heat capacity (J/kg°C), ΔT = temperature change

Specific heat capacities:
Water: 4200 J/kg°C (very high — why oceans moderate climate)
Aluminium: 900 J/kg°C
Copper: 385 J/kg°C
Iron: 450 J/kg°C

content.title: Worked Example
content.body:
How much energy is needed to heat 2 kg of water from 20°C to 100°C?
Given: m = 2 kg, c = 4200 J/kg°C, ΔT = 100 − 20 = 80°C
Q = mcΔT = 2 × 4200 × 80 = 672,000 J = 672 kJ

Specific Latent Heat:
Energy needed to change state WITHOUT changing temperature
Q = mL
L = specific latent heat (J/kg)

Latent heat of fusion (melting/freezing): water = 334,000 J/kg
Latent heat of vaporisation (boiling/condensing): water = 2,260,000 J/kg

questions.question: Convert 25°C to Kelvin.
What is absolute zero?
questions.explanation: Absolute zero is 0 K = −273°C. At this temperature, particles have minimum possible kinetic energy.

questions.question: The formula Q = mcΔT is used to calculate...
questions.explanation: Q = mcΔT calculates the heat energy needed to change the temperature of mass m by ΔT, given specific heat capacity c.

questions.question: How much energy heats 3 kg of copper (c=385 J/kg°C) by 40°C?
Why does water have a moderating effect on coastal climates?
options: It has a very high specific heat capacity
It has a low boiling point
questions.answer: It has a very high specific heat capacity
questions.explanation: Water's high specific heat capacity (4200 J/kg°C) means it absorbs and releases large amounts of energy with little temperature change, moderating coastal temperatures.

questions.question: What is specific latent heat?
options: Energy to change state of 1 kg without temperature change
questions.answer: Energy to change state of 1 kg without temperature change
questions.explanation: Specific latent heat is the energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without any change in temperature.

questions.question: What happens to temperature during melting?
questions.explanation: During melting, energy goes into breaking bonds between particles, not increasing kinetic energy. Temperature stays constant.

questions.question: The latent heat of vaporisation of water is much larger than its latent heat of fusion because...
options: More bonds must be completely broken when boiling than melting
Steam needs more energy to form
questions.answer: More bonds must be completely broken when boiling than melting
questions.explanation: During vaporisation, all intermolecular bonds are broken. During melting, bonds are only weakened. Hence vaporisation requires much more energy.

questions.question: What is the energy needed to melt 0.5 kg of ice? (L = 334,000 J/kg)
Temperature is a measure of...
questions.explanation: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. Higher temperature = faster moving particles.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: The behaviour of gases can be described by three gas laws, each relating two variables while keeping the third constant. These laws combine into the ideal gas law, which describes the behaviour of an ideal gas.

Boyle's Law (constant temperature):
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
Pressure and volume are inversely proportional at constant temperature

Charles' Law (constant pressure):
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
Volume is directly proportional to temperature (in Kelvin) at constant pressure

Pressure Law (constant volume):
P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂
Pressure is directly proportional to temperature (in Kelvin) at constant volume

Combined Gas Law:
P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂

Ideal Gas Equation:
PV = nRT
P = pressure (Pa), V = volume (m³), n = number of moles
R = 8.31 J/mol/K (universal gas constant), T = temperature (K)

content.title: Boyle's Law Example
content.body:
A gas occupies 4 L at 200 kPa. What is the volume at 800 kPa (temperature constant)?
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
200 × 4 = 800 × V₂
V₂ = 800/800 = 1 L
The volume decreases as pressure increases.

content.title: Charles' Law Example
content.body:
A gas occupies 3 L at 27°C. What is the volume at 127°C (pressure constant)?
First convert: T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K, T₂ = 127 + 273 = 400 K
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
3/300 = V₂/400
V₂ = 3 × 400/300 = 4 L

questions.question: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, pressure and volume are...
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: PV = constant. If pressure doubles, volume halves. Pressure and volume are inversely proportional.

questions.question: In gas law calculations, temperature must be in...
questions.explanation: Gas laws use absolute temperature in Kelvin. Using Celsius would give incorrect results because the scale doesn't start at zero energy.

questions.question: A gas is at 2 atm and 6 L. Pressure increases to 3 atm (constant T). New volume?
questions.explanation: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ → 2×6 = 3×V₂ → V₂ = 12/3 = 4 L

questions.question: What does Charles' Law state?
options: P is proportional to T at constant V
V is proportional to T at constant P
P is inversely proportional to V
questions.answer: V is proportional to T at constant P
questions.explanation: Charles' Law: V/T = constant (at constant pressure). Volume increases proportionally with absolute temperature.

questions.question: A gas is at 300 K and 2 L. Temperature rises to 600 K (constant pressure). New volume?
questions.explanation: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ → 2/300 = V₂/600 → V₂ = 4 L. Volume doubles when absolute temperature doubles.

questions.question: In PV = nRT, what does R represent?
questions.explanation: R is the universal (molar) gas constant with value 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

questions.question: At constant volume, if temperature doubles (in K), pressure...
questions.explanation: Pressure Law: P/T = constant. If temperature doubles, pressure doubles.

questions.question: Convert 0°C to Kelvin for use in gas law calculations.
Which law is described by P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂?
questions.explanation: The combined gas law merges Boyle's, Charles', and the Pressure Law into one equation.

questions.question: An ideal gas has 2 moles at 300 K and 1000 Pa. What is the volume? (R=8.31)
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

lessons.title: Heat Transfer — Conduction, Convection and Radiation

content.body: Heat energy can be transferred from one place to another by three mechanisms: conduction, convection, and radiation. In real situations, all three may occur simultaneously. Understanding these helps explain many everyday phenomena.

content.title: Conduction
content.body:
Heat transfer through a material by particle vibration — no bulk movement of matter.
Best in solids, especially metals.
Mechanism in metals: free electrons carry energy rapidly.
Mechanism in non-metals: vibrating particles pass energy to neighbours.

Good conductors: copper, aluminium, iron
Poor conductors (insulators): wood, plastic, air, glass, rubber

Applications: cooking pans (metal base), heat sinks in electronics

content.title: Convection
content.body:
Heat transfer by bulk movement of a fluid (liquid or gas).
Occurs only in liquids and gases.
Mechanism: Hot fluid expands, becomes less dense, rises. Cool fluid sinks. Creates a convection current.

Applications:
• Sea breezes (land heats faster than sea)
• Hot water heating systems
• Weather patterns and trade winds
• Refrigerators (cooling element at top)

Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation):
Power radiated: P = εσAT⁴
ε = emissivity (0-1), σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²/K⁴
A = surface area, T = temperature in K

Black body: perfect absorber and emitter (ε = 1)
A hotter object emits more radiation and at shorter wavelengths

content.title: Radiation
content.body:
Transfer of energy by electromagnetic waves (infrared).
Does NOT need a medium — can travel through vacuum.
All objects emit thermal radiation.
Dark, matt surfaces: best absorbers and emitters
Light, shiny surfaces: best reflectors, poorest emitters

Applications:
• Solar panels (dark surface)
• Thermos flask (silvered walls reduce radiation)
• Greenhouse effect
• Infrared thermometers

questions.question: Which method of heat transfer does NOT require a medium?
questions.explanation: Radiation transfers heat as electromagnetic waves (infrared) and can travel through a vacuum. Conduction and convection need matter.

questions.question: Why are metals good conductors of heat?
options: They have free electrons that transfer energy quickly
questions.answer: They have free electrons that transfer energy quickly
questions.explanation: Metals have free (delocalised) electrons that can move rapidly through the metal, carrying thermal energy with them.

questions.question: Convection occurs in...
questions.explanation: Convection requires bulk movement of matter. Liquids and gases can flow, but solids cannot — so convection only occurs in fluids.

questions.question: Why does hot air rise?
options: Hot air expands and becomes less dense
questions.answer: Hot air expands and becomes less dense
questions.explanation: When air is heated, it expands and its density decreases. Less dense hot air rises above the denser cool air below it.

questions.question: Which surface is the best emitter of infrared radiation?
questions.explanation: Dull, dark (black) surfaces are the best absorbers and emitters of infrared radiation. Shiny surfaces reflect radiation.

questions.question: A thermos flask reduces heat loss by...
options: Silvered walls (reduce radiation) and vacuum (no conduction/convection)
questions.answer: Silvered walls (reduce radiation) and vacuum (no conduction/convection)
questions.explanation: The silvered walls reflect radiation back in. The vacuum between walls prevents conduction and convection.

questions.question: Which law gives radiation power as P = εσAT⁴?
questions.explanation: The Stefan-Boltzmann Law relates the power radiated by an object to its temperature: P = εσAT⁴.

questions.question: A sea breeze occurs because...
options: Land heats faster than sea, causing convection
questions.answer: Land heats faster than sea, causing convection
questions.explanation: Land heats faster than sea. Hot air rises over land, cool air from the sea moves in to replace it — creating a sea breeze by convection.

questions.question: If temperature doubles (in K), radiation emitted changes by a factor of...
questions.explanation: By Stefan-Boltzmann: P ∝ T⁴. If T doubles, P increases by 2⁴ = 16 times.

questions.question: Wood is a poor conductor of heat because...
options: It has no free electrons and particles are not closely packed
questions.answer: It has no free electrons and particles are not closely packed
questions.explanation: Wood is an insulator because it lacks free electrons (the main carrier of heat in metals) and has a less ordered structure.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
1st Law: ΔU = Q - W
ΔU = change in internal energy
Q = heat added to system
W = work done BY system

2nd Law: heat flows naturally from hot to cold
Entropy S always increases in isolated system

Efficiency of heat engine:
η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H
Carnot efficiency (max): η = 1 - T_C/T_H (temperatures in Kelvin)

Work done by gas: W = pΔV (constant pressure)

content.title: 1st Law Examples
content.body:
Gas heated: Q=500J added, gas expands doing W=200J
ΔU = 500-200 = 300J (internal energy rises)

Adiabatic process: Q=0, ΔU=-W
Isothermal: T constant, ΔU=0, Q=W
Isochoric (constant V): W=0, ΔU=Q

content.title: Carnot Engine
content.body:
Steam engine: T_H=500K, T_C=300K
Max efficiency = 1-300/500 = 1-0.6 = 40%
Means even ideal engine wastes 60% of heat
Real engines always less efficient than Carnot

questions.question: 1st law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W means
options: change in internal energy = heat added minus work done by system
questions.answer: change in internal energy = heat added minus work done by system
questions.explanation: Energy conservation: internal energy increases by heat added, decreases by work done by the system

questions.question: In isothermal process (T constant)
questions.explanation: Isothermal: T constant → internal energy constant (for ideal gas) → all heat input converts to work

questions.question: Adiabatic process: Q=0. Therefore
questions.explanation: Q=0: 1st law gives ΔU=0-W=-W. Gas does work at expense of its own internal energy

questions.question: 2nd law says entropy in isolated system
questions.explanation: 2nd law: total entropy of universe always increases; heat flows hot→cold spontaneously

questions.question: Carnot efficiency η = 1 - T_C/T_H. T_H=600K, T_C=300K. η =
Real engine is always less efficient than Carnot because
options: friction, turbulence, heat losses mean irreversibilities
questions.answer: friction, turbulence, heat losses mean irreversibilities
questions.explanation: Carnot is idealized reversible engine. Real processes are irreversible (friction generates entropy)

questions.question: Work done by expanding gas at constant pressure p, volume change ΔV =
questions.explanation: W=pΔV for isobaric (constant pressure) expansion

questions.question: Refrigerator moves heat from cold to hot. This
options: satisfies 2nd law only with work input
questions.answer: satisfies 2nd law only with work input
questions.explanation: Heat doesn't flow cold→hot spontaneously. Refrigerator does work to force this transfer

questions.question: A heat pump has COP = Q_H/W. T_H=300K, T_C=270K. Max COP =
questions.explanation: Carnot COP_HP = T_H/(T_H-T_C) = 300/30 = 10

questions.question: Entropy is related to
questions.explanation: S=k_B ln(W). More microstates = higher entropy = more disorder

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Thermal Physics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Thermal Physics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Thermal Physics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Thermal Physics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Thermal Physics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Thermal Physics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Thermal Physics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
